The real shift: outsourcing may move from labor-cost arbitrage (India, Philippines) to capability arbitrage (specialized talent clusters). talking_points: AI doesn't uniformly kill or accelerate outsourcing; it kills *wage-based* outsourcing for automatable work while strengthening *capability-based* outsourcing for skills AI can't replace.

For fintech and specialty finance, the talent play shifts from cheap labor pools to clusters (SF, NYC, London, Tel Aviv) where hard-to-automate domain expertise concentrates—affecting where PE and banks scout acquisition targets.
summary_ai.highlight: Outsourcing may be reshaping from labor-cost arbitrage to capability arbitrage—a shift that reframes consolidation and build-vs-buy decisions for financial services.

summary_ai.summary_long: A QJE paper traces the persistent US racial wealth gap to slavery-era initial conditions, proposing that compounding effects over 150 years lock in historical disadvantage. Cowen's critique focuses on a pressure test: millions of white immigrants arrived with zero inherited wealth during 1850–1924, yet did not see their descendants' wealth converge downward to Black Americans' level. This suggests either (a) initial conditions are not the dominant mechanism, or (b) post-slavery discrimination and exclusion operated with sufficient force to overcome the leveling effect of mass immigration. The debate hinges on what explains persistent wealth stratification when one group started with nothing.
talking_points: If slavery-era wealth gaps fully explained today's divergence, white immigrant descendants—who started as poor as freed slaves—should have converged to similar wealth levels by now.

The paper's logic implies compounding differences over 150 years dominate near-term economic mobility, but the immigration counterexample suggests active exclusion (housing, credit, hiring discrimination) may matter more than path dependency alone.
This reframes the wealth-gap debate: is it about initial conditions or about continuous friction that prevents convergence even when starting positions equalize?
summary_ai.highlight: The immigration pressure test: if initial conditions explain the gap, why didn't poor white immigrants' descendants converge downward to Black wealth levels?
